perturbation.original_text:
Confidential Information means all confidential information (however recorded, preserved or disclosed) disclosed by a party or its Representatives to the other party a nd that party's Representatives including but not limited to:
(a) The fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations;
(b) The existence and terms of this Agreement;
(c) Any information that would be regarded as confidential by a reasonable business person relating to:
(i) The business, affairs, custo mers, clients, SAMPLE2 suppliers, plans , inte ntions, or market opportunities of the Disclosing Party; and
(ii) The operations, processes, product information, know -how, designs, trade secrets or s oftware of the Disclosing Party; and
(d) Any information or analysis derived from Confidential Information;
but not including any information that:
(e) Is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of its disclosure by the Recipient or its Representa tives in breach of this Agreement or of any other undertaking of confidentiality addressed to the party to whom the information relates (except that any compilation of otherwise public information in a form not publicly known shall nevertheless be treated as Confidential Information); or
(f) Was available to the Recipient on a non - confidential basis prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; or
(g) Was lawfully in the possession of the Recipient before the information was disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party; or
(h) The parties agree in writing is not confidential or may be disclosed .
perturbation.changed_text:
Confidential Information means only that information explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in writing at the time of disclosure. This includes but is not limited to:
(a) The fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations; if explicitly marked as 'Confidential'.
(b) The existence and terms of this Agreement; if explicitly marked as 'Confidential'.
(c) Any information that would be regarded as confidential by a reasonable business person relating to:
(i) The business, affairs, custo mers, clients, SAMPLE2 suppliers, plans , inte ntions, or market opportunities of the Disclosing Party; only if explicitly marked as 'Confidential'; and
(ii) The operations, processes, product information, know -how, designs, trade secrets or s oftware of the Disclosing Party; only if explicitly marked as 'Confidential'; and
(d) Any information or analysis derived from Confidential Information; if explicitly marked as 'Confidential'.
but not including any information that:
(e) Is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of its disclosure by the Recipient or its Representa tives in breach of this Agreement or of any other undertaking of confidentiality addressed to the party to whom the information relates (except that any compilation of otherwise public information in a form not publicly known shall nevertheless be treated as Confidential Information); or
(f) Was available to the Recipient on a non - confidential basis prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; or
(g) Was lawfully in the possession of the Recipient before the information was disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party; or
(h) The parties agree in writing is not confidential or may be disclosed .
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Confidential Information' is broad, encompassing information 'however recorded, preserved or disclosed.' The modified version narrows the definition to include only information 'explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in writing at the time of disclosure.' This contradicts the original intent and creates ambiguity. This directly contradicts section 4.1 where "All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party.", now the question arrises is, is the information confidential, given the narrow defition now in place?
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Each party reserves all rights in its Confidential Information. No rights, including, but not limited to, intellectual property rights, in respect of a party's Confidential Information are granted to the other party and no obligations are imposed on the Disclosing Party other than those expressly stated in this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Confidential Information' broadly. The changed text significantly narrows this definition, requiring explicit written marking. This contradicts the contradicted text, which states 'All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party.' The narrowed definition introduces uncertainty about what qualifies as 'Confidential Information,' thus conflicting with the statement that all such information remains the property of the disclosing party, and makes the contract internally inconsistent and potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, shall: (a) Warrants and undertake that it is solely responsible for ensuring that the Personal Data is processed by it in accordance with the Data Protection Act 1998 (“the Act”) from the date that it is received from the Disclosing Party; (b) Not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose. If the Recipient proposes to use or process the Personal Data for a purpose other than the Purpose, the Recipient shall ensure that, prior to using or processing the Personal Data in such manner, it will ensure that such use or process is in compliance with the Act; (c) Not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement; and (d) Not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except as strictly necessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Disclosing Party).
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential, however, the Recipient is allowed to share the Confidential Information with anyone, without written permission of the disclosing party. The Recipient will be held responsible for ensuring that the Personal Data is processed by it in accordance with the Data Protection Act 1998 (“the Act”) from the date that it is received from the Disclosing Party; (b) The Recipient can use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way they choose, even outside of the original purpose. If the Recipient proposes to use or process the Personal Data for a purpose other than the Purpose, the Recipient shall ensure that, prior to using or processing the Personal Data in such manner, it will ensure that such use or process is in compliance with the Act; (c) The Recipient can disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, even not expressly permitted by this Agreement; and (d) The Recipient can copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information without any limits. Such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause strictly limits the Recipient's use and disclosure of Confidential Information. The altered clause removes these restrictions, now allowing the Recipient to share, exploit, and copy the information without consent. This directly contradicts the original purpose of a Non-Disclosure Agreement, in the title of the document and background context, and undermines the Disclosing Party's protection of their information, which is the reason for the agreement in the first place, the second clause also mentions the obligations of confidetiality, this perturbation removes all the obligations. This changes and creates an in-text contradiction
perturbation.contradicted_text: OBLIGATIONS OF CONFIDENTIALITY 2.1 The Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, shall: (a) Warrants and undertake that it is solely responsible for ensuring that the Personal Data is processed by it in accordance with the Data Protection Act 1998 (“the Act”) from the date that it is received from the Disclosing Party; (b) Not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose. If the Recipient proposes to use or process the Personal Data for a purpose other than the Purpose, the Recipient shall ensure that, prior to using or processing the Personal Data in such manner, it will ensure that such use or process is in compliance with the Act; (c) Not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement; and (d) Not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except as strictly necessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Disclosing Party).
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly allows the Recipient to share, exploit, and copy the Confidential Information without the Disclosing Party's consent. This directly contradicts the 'OBLIGATIONS OF CONFIDENTIALITY' outlined in the contradicted text, which strictly prohibits such actions. The changed text removes all confidentiality obligations, whereas the contradicted text establishes them, causing a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall: (a) Destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (b) Erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information from its computer systems or which is stored in electronic form (to the extent possible); and (c) Certify in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this clause, provided that a Recipient may retain documents and materials containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to the extent required by law or any applicable governmental or regulatory authority and to the extent reasonable to permit the Recipient to keep evidence that it has performed its obligations under this Agreement. The provisions of this Agreement shall continue to apply to any documents and materials retained by the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient may choose to: (a) Destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; only if Recipient deems it fair. (b) Erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information from its computer systems or which is stored in electronic form (to the extent possible); the Recipient will only do this if it is not too much effort; and (c) The Recipient is not required to Certify in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this clause. The Recipient is allowed to retain documents and materials containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information no matter what, there are no constrains for the Recipient. The provisions of this Agreement are now optional to any documents and materials retained by the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates the Recipient to return or destroy Confidential Information upon request. The revised clause makes this action optional ('may choose to'), introduces subjective conditions ('only if Recipient deems it fair,' 'if it is not too much effort'), and removes the certification requirement. This contradicts the core obligation to protect confidentiality and weakens the Disclosing Party's control over their information. this changes and creates an in-text contradiction
perturbation.contradicted_text: RETURN OF INFORMATION 3.1 At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall: (a) Destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (b) Erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information from its computer systems or which is stored in electronic form (to the extent possible); and (c) Certify in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this clause, provided that a Recipient may retain documents and materials containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to the extent required by law or any applicable governmental or regulatory authority and to the extent reasonable to permit the Recipient to keep evidence that it has performed its obligations under this Agreement. The provisions of this Agreement shall continue to apply to any documents and materials retained by the Recipient.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly alters the Recipient's obligations regarding the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information. The original text mandates the return or destruction of confidential information upon request, whereas the changed text makes this action optional and introduces subjective conditions like 'only if Recipient deems it fair' and 'if it is not too much effort.' Additionally, it removes the requirement for the Recipient to certify compliance. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text,' which explicitly states the Recipient 'shall' return or destroy the information and 'shall' certify compliance. This creates a clear and undeniable conflict within the document, making the initial obligation unenforceable, therefore 'YES'.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Each party reserves all rights in its Confidential Information. No rights, including, but not limited to, intellectual property rights, in respect of a party's Confidential Information are granted to the other party and no obligations are imposed on the Disclosing Party other than those expressly stated in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information is now the shared property of both the Disclosing Party and the Recipient. Both parties now share equal rights in the Confidential Information. Rights, including intellectual property rights, regarding a party's Confidential Information are implicitly granted to the other party, even if not explicitly stated in this Agreement. All obligations are imposed on the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause asserts that Confidential Information remains the sole property of the Disclosing Party. The altered version states that the information becomes shared property, granting the Recipient equal rights, including implicit intellectual property rights. This contradicts the fundamental premise of an NDA, which is to protect the Disclosing Party's exclusive ownership and control of their confidential information, section 4.4 the remedy for breach of contract, now the property is shared, so there's not that much of a breach of contract anymore. this changes and creates an in-text contradiction
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient acknowledges that damages alone would not be an adequate remedy for the breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement. Accordingly, without prejudice to any other rights and remedies it may have, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to the granting of equitable relief (including without limitation injunctive relief) concerning any threatened or actual breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that Confidential Information becomes the shared property of both parties, granting the Recipient equal rights, including implicit intellectual property rights. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which provides that the Disclosing Party is entitled to equitable relief for breaches of the agreement. If the Confidential Information is now shared property, the remedy for breach of contract related to that information becomes significantly undermined, because the Disclosing Party no longer has exclusive ownership, creating a contradiction between the rights and remedies available.

perturbation.original_text: Each Disclosing Party warrants that it has the right to disclose its Confidential Information to the Recipient and to authorise the Recipient to use such Confidential Information for the Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Disclosing Party makes no warranty whatsoever regarding its right to disclose any information, confidential or otherwise, to the Recipient. Furthermore, the Disclosing Party explicitly denies authorizing the Recipient to use any information received for any purpose whatsoever. The Recipient acknowledges that the Disclosing Party might not have complete control over the information being shared and accepts full responsibility for its use of said information.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states that the Disclosing Party warrants they have the right to disclose the information and authorize its use for the stated purpose. The revised clause negates this warranty, stating that the Disclosing Party makes no such guarantee and denies authorization. This undermines the entire basis of the NDA, implying the Disclosing Party may not even have the right to share the information or control how it's used. This is contradicting the purpose of a NDA, that it has the right to disclose its Confidetial Information, to be considered Confidential the party must have the right, else anyone can just use it. This creates an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: WARRANTY AND INDEMNITY 5.1 Each Disclosing Party warrants that it has the right to disclose its Confidential Information to the Recipient and to authorise the Recipient to use such Confidential Information for the Purpose. 5.2 Each Recipient shall indemnify and keep fully indemnified the Disclosing Party at all times against all liabilities, costs (including legal costs on an indemnity basis), expenses, damages and losses (including any direct, indirect or consequential losses, loss of profit, loss of reputation and all interest, penalties and other reasonable costs and expenses suffered or incurred by the Disclosing Party) arising from any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and from the actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the contradicted text. The original text and the contradicted text (which are the same in this case) state that the Disclosing Party warrants they have the right to disclose information and authorize its use. The changed text explicitly denies any such warranty or authorization. This creates a direct conflict and inconsistency, making the obligations of the parties unclear and potentially unenforceable. This is a strong in-text contradiction as per the 'Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of each party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiations or discussions between the parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of two (2) years from the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of each party shall immediately cease upon termination of negotiations or discussions between the parties in relation to the Purpose, effectively voiding any further confidentiality requirements. Therefore, if the parties decide to not continue any negotiations, the confidentiality agreement is void immediately.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies that confidentiality obligations continue for two years after the agreement is terminated. The altered version states that these obligations cease immediately upon termination of negotiations, creating a direct contradiction regarding the duration of confidentiality. This undermines the long-term protection of Confidential Information. Now, Section 6.2 states that the Termination of the agreemen shall not affect any accrued rights or remedies to which either party is entitled, but since the termination is immediate, there is no time to have accrued rights to the parties. This creates an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Termination of this Agreement shall not affect any accrued rights or remedies to which either party is entitled.
perturbation.justification: The changed text states that obligations cease immediately upon termination of negotiations, while the contradicted text asserts that termination does not affect accrued rights. This creates a direct conflict because if all obligations are immediately ceased, no rights can accrue after negotiations end, as stated in the explanation, therefore, creating a strong in-text contradiction.
